pathophysiology_summary: Tinea corporis is a dermatophyte infection of glabrous skin (here zoophilic, from a new kitten) producing annular, pruritic, erythematous plaques with a raised scaly advancing (active) border, central clearing and sometimes pustules at the margin, expanding centrifugally. Topical corticosteroid (OTC hydrocortisone) suppresses local immunity and accelerates/atypically modifies the spread — 'tinea incognito' — so it must be stopped. Discriminators: a prominent scaly, pruritic advancing border with central clearing distinguishes it from the smooth, non-scaly, usually asymptomatic expanding rash of erythema migrans (Lyme); central clearing with an active raised edge distinguishes it from uniformly inflamed nummular eczema; and marginal pustules with animal contact and asymmetry argue against pityriasis rosea. Management is stopping the steroid, a topical antifungal (terbinafine) applied to and ~2 cm beyond the border, continued ~1–2 weeks past clearance, itch relief, hygiene/no towel sharing, and checking the kitten; a short oral antifungal is reserved for extensive/refractory/steroid-modified disease. Safety-net for secondary bacterial infection.

differential.evidence_basis: Annular scaly advancing-border plaques with central clearing after kitten contact.
An expanding annular rash — but EM is smooth/non-scaly and usually not itchy.
Coin-shaped uniformly inflamed plaques without central clearing.
Herald patch/Christmas-tree pattern, not annular scaly rings with marginal pustules.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise tinea corporis (annular scaly advancing border), stop the steroid, and treat with antifungal extended past the border; keep EM/Lyme on the differential briefly.

differential.evidence_basis: Scaly advancing border with central clearing, steroid-modified; no secondary infection.
Scaly pruritic border with central clearing is not EM (smooth/non-scaly).
Working diagnosis: stop steroid, topical antifungal past the border (± short oral if extensive), itch relief, hygiene, check the kitten; infection safety-net.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection (hot/hard swelling, thick pus, fever)

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not stopping the topical steroid that drives tinea incognito.
Not applying antifungal beyond the visible border, allowing continued spread.
Not advising a vet check for the kitten (reinfection).

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Stop the steroid, prescribe a topical antifungal to/beyond the border, itch relief, hygiene and vet check; short oral antifungal if extensive/refractory; review/urgent care if secondary infection.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Continuing steroids (tinea incognito) or missing secondary infection.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Tinea corporis: topical antifungal (e.g., terbinafine) to and beyond the active border; oral antifungal for extensive/refractory/steroid-modified disease; stop topical corticosteroids; treat sources/contacts.
